title: Help and support for people with dementia - overview for carers
content: After a dementia diagnosis, the person you care for should be given a written care plan that sets out what support they and their carers might need and how to keep doing things that matter to them for as long as possible. This plan should signpost services, list any other health conditions that need regular checks, and name a professional who will coordinate support, and it should be reviewed at least once a year. Local services such as memory assessment services, the council, or the GP usually help create and update this plan, though the exact setup varies by area. If day-to-day tasks like washing, dressing or cooking are becoming difficult, you can request a social care needs assessment from your local council; this is usually done face to face and you can bring a relative or friend for support and note-taking. If the assessment finds that help is needed, such as carers, meals on wheels, or a personal alarm, the council will then carry out a financial assessment to work out how much must be paid towards the care. If your relative’s needs are mainly health-related, they may be eligible for free NHS continuing healthcare, which is assessed by NHS staff.
source: NHS Help and support for people with dementia

title: Support, assessments and care options for dementia carers
content: If you are caring for someone with dementia, you may be entitled to Carer’s Allowance and support from your local council, but before you receive help from the council you should request a carer’s assessment to discuss your own needs. A carer’s assessment can identify practical help, respite options and other support to help you keep caring safely. Many people with mild to moderate dementia can stay in their own home if they have enough support, and familiar surroundings can help them cope better. Over time, symptoms usually worsen and some people will need to move into a residential or nursing home that can support people with dementia; this decision can be difficult, so it helps to talk it through with family and friends and consider a trial stay if possible. Before a move, the local council must do another needs assessment and a financial assessment to decide how much the person will need to pay towards care home fees. Care homes in England are inspected by the Care Quality Commission (CQC), and families can read inspection reports to help choose a suitable home.
source: NHS Help and support for people with dementia
licence: check before redistribution

title: Charities, helplines and peer support for dementia carers
content: Several charities provide information, emotional support and practical advice for people with dementia and their carers. Alzheimer’s Society offers information on all types of dementia, advice on living with the condition, and details of local support, and runs the Dementia Support Line on 0333 150 3456 for information and advice. Dementia UK is a specialist dementia nursing charity that supports families and runs the Admiral Nurse Dementia Helpline (0800 888 6678, or email helpline@dementiauk.org) for carers, people with dementia and professionals. Alzheimer’s Research UK focuses on research but also answers questions about dementia and how families can get involved, via its helpline on 0300 111 5 111. Age UK provides advice on advance care planning, benefits, and choosing a care home, as well as local activities and services, and offers a free advice line on 0800 055 6112. Carers Trust and Carers UK both provide information on carers’ rights, benefits and practical support, including how to get help for yourself as a carer. Online forums such as the Alzheimer’s Society Dementia Support Forum and the Carers UK forum allow carers and people with dementia to share experiences and support each other.
source: NHS Help and support for people with dementia
licence: check before redistribution

title: Information resources and emails for dementia carers
content: Reading Well provides a curated list of books to support people with dementia, their relatives and carers, and those who want to understand the condition better. GPs and other healthcare professionals can recommend titles from this list, and the books are available free to borrow from local libraries. The NHS Dementia Information Service offers a structured email series to guide people through the early period after a dementia diagnosis. By signing up, you receive weekly emails for six weeks, each covering a different essential topic, helping carers and families understand what to expect and where to find support. These resources can complement advice from professionals and charities by providing reliable information that you can read and revisit in your own time.
source: NHS Help and support for people with dementia
licence: check before redistribution

title: What to expect when seeing a GP about dementia concerns
content: This page explains what usually happens when someone sees a GP because they are worried about their memory or possible dementia. It stresses that it can help to encourage a loved one with memory problems to book a GP appointment and to offer to go with them so you can share what you have noticed and help them remember what was discussed. The GP will ask about current symptoms and general health, and will want to know if everyday tasks such as washing and dressing, cooking and shopping, or paying bills have become more difficult. They may invite someone who knows the person well to describe any changes in behaviour, memory, or daily functioning, as this gives a fuller picture than relying on the person’s memory alone. The page emphasises safe uncertainty by stating that memory problems do not always mean dementia, and can be caused by things like depression, anxiety, sudden confusion from illness, an underactive thyroid, or side effects of some medicines. To rule out other causes, the GP will do a physical examination and may arrange blood and urine tests, as well as a simple memory or thinking test, and will then decide whether to refer on for specialist assessment.
source: NHS - How to get a dementia diagnosis

title: Why GPs involve family and what they may check
content: The NHS guidance explains that GPs often encourage the person to bring someone who knows them well, such as a family member or carer, to appointments about memory concerns. This companion can describe changes in memory, mood, or everyday abilities that the person might not notice or might forget to mention, and can help recall what the GP said afterward. During the appointment, the GP will ask about difficulties with personal care like washing and dressing, managing cooking and shopping, and handling money or bills, to understand how memory or thinking issues are affecting daily life. They will also ask about wider health and emotional wellbeing because problems such as depression, anxiety, delirium from a medical condition, an underactive thyroid, or medicine side effects can cause memory and thinking problems that are not dementia. To check for these, the GP may examine the person and arrange blood tests and a urine test, and they will usually carry out a brief memory or cognitive test to see how well the person can remember, concentrate, and think clearly. If these checks suggest dementia and other causes have been ruled out, the GP may refer to a specialist service, such as a memory clinic, for more detailed assessment while still keeping the focus on support rather than making carers feel they need to diagnose the condition themselves.
source: NHS - How to get a dementia diagnosis
licence: Open Government Licence v3.0, with NHS website exceptions

title: What sudden confusion looks like and why it matters
content: Sudden confusion (also called delirium) is different from gradual memory or thinking changes that develop slowly over time. If someone suddenly becomes confused, it is a medical emergency that needs immediate attention. A person with sudden confusion may not be able to think or speak clearly, may not know where they are, may struggle to pay attention or remember things, or may see or hear things that are not there. To check if someone needs help, try asking their name, age, and today's date—if they seem unsure or cannot answer, they probably need medical help right away. While gradual confusion or forgetfulness that gets worse over time could be a sign of dementia, sudden confusion is an acute change that requires urgent assessment. Many causes of sudden confusion can be life-threatening and need treatment as soon as possible. Go to A&E or call 999 for an ambulance if someone suddenly becomes confused.
source: NHS / South London and Maudsley
licence: check before redistribution

title: What to do while waiting for help
content: If someone suddenly becomes confused, call 999 for an ambulance and stay with them while you wait. Keep the person safe and reassured by staying calm, telling them who you are and where they are, and repeating this reassurance as needed. Use simple words and short sentences when you speak to them. If possible, make a note of any medicines they are taking so you can share this information with the paramedics or doctors. While you wait, do not ask lots of questions, as this may increase distress or overwhelm the person. Do not try to stop them from moving around unless they are in immediate danger. The goal is to keep them safe, calm, and reassured until medical help arrives. Remember, sudden confusion is a medical emergency—do not wait to see if it gets better on its own.
source: NHS / South London and Maudsley
licence: check before redistribution

title: Common causes of sudden confusion in older people and people with dementia
content: Sudden confusion can be caused by many different things, and it is important not to try to self-diagnose. However, understanding common causes can help caregivers recognise why sudden confusion is serious. In elderly people and people with dementia, urinary tract infections (UTIs) are a very common cause of sudden confusion. Other possible causes include stroke or mini-stroke (TIA), low blood sugar in people with diabetes, head injury, some types of prescription medicines, alcohol poisoning or withdrawal, taking illegal drugs, carbon monoxide poisoning (especially if other people in the home also become unwell), severe asthma attacks or other lung or heart problems, and certain types of seizures. Each of these conditions requires urgent medical assessment and treatment. If someone with dementia suddenly becomes more confused than usual, or their confusion feels different from their normal pattern, do not assume it is just their dementia getting worse—it may be an acute medical problem that needs emergency care.
source: NHS / South London and Maudsley

title: What is delirium and how does it affect someone with dementia?
content: Delirium is sudden confusion that usually affects people's brains for a short time. It is common: up to 1 in 3 people admitted to hospital become delirious during their stay. People with delirium might be confused, agitated, or act differently than usual. They can seem alert or drowsy (sleepy), have memory problems, or see and hear things that are not there. Delirium can be frightening for the person and their family. Not everyone remembers it, but those that do may find the memories distressing. Older people and those diagnosed with dementia are particularly at risk. People over 80 years old with dementia are especially vulnerable, but delirium can happen in younger people too, particularly if they are critically ill or recovering from surgery. Delirium can be treated at hospital or managed at home depending on the severity. It usually gets better: in 6 out of 10 people the symptoms disappear within 6 days, though some people may have symptoms for longer. About 1 in 20 people may still have delirium more than a month after first symptoms. Although many people make a full recovery, some people never get back to how they were completely, particularly those who already have dementia, have had delirium before, or live in a care home.
source: Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust
licence: check before redistribution

title: How to recognise delirium: signs and symptoms for carers
content: Family members and carers are often the first to notice small changes that signal delirium. Key symptoms include disorientation (not knowing where they are or what time it is, not recognising family), unusual thoughts (becoming paranoid, suspicious, or mistrustful), poor concentration, memory loss (especially short-term memory), and sleepiness or drowsiness. Some people may become agitated, restless, or aggressive, may shout, or get out of bed unexpectedly, increasing the risk of falling. Hallucinations may occur, where the person sees and hears things that are not there. Delirium can change rapidly within a single day: a person may be delirious, then appear normal, then be delirious again. Symptoms are often worse at night. Physical changes can include changes in appetite, movement, or swallowing, and increased unsteadiness or falls. The person may not realise anything is wrong. If you notice someone has become very confused after a hip operation, is pulling out their drip, shouting at nurses, or is suddenly not recognising family despite usually managing well with mild dementia at home, these are signs of delirium. If someone is sleeping much more, getting slower, becoming quiet and withdrawn, or wandering at night trying to leave, these may also be delirium symptoms.
source: Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust

title: What to do if you think someone has delirium
content: If you notice symptoms of delirium, it is important to tell a healthcare professional. In hospital, tell the nurse or doctor looking after the person about the symptoms you have noticed. At home, talk to a GP or contact community nurses. The person with delirium may not know that anything is wrong, so family and friends often need to raise the concern. Doctors and nurses will test for and manage any treatable causes of delirium, such as infections, abnormal blood results, constipation, not being able to pee, dehydration, or side effects from medicines. Sometimes calming or sedating medicines are used if the person is still distressed or unsafe after other methods have been tried. When someone leaves hospital after delirium, they may need more support than usual and be at higher risk of falls. Changes in the home may be needed to make the environment safe. If extra support is needed, speak to the ward doctors and nurses before the person goes home. Support can include rehabilitation to improve everyday skills and mobility. If you are concerned after going home, speak to staff at an outpatient appointment or contact the GP. Guy's and St Thomas' delirium and dementia team can be reached Monday to Friday, 9am to 5pm, or out of hours via the site nurse practitioner.
source: Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust
licence: check before redistribution

title: Practical support for someone with delirium
content: If someone is suddenly more confused or has delirium-like symptoms, you can offer simple, steady support alongside seeking medical advice when needed. Stay calm and speak in short, clear sentences, allowing extra time for the person to understand and answer. Offer gentle reassurance, acknowledge that they may feel frightened, and let them know you are there to help. If they seem unsure where they are or what is happening, quietly remind them of the place, the day, and why they are there. Make sure they can see a clock or calendar, and point these out when you remind them about the date and time. Check that they are wearing their usual glasses and hearing aids so they can see and hear as well as possible. Help them drink regularly and encourage small amounts of food, as hospital staff may also be doing this. Try to have someone they know well with them, especially in the evening when confusion can often feel worse. Bringing in familiar objects from home, such as photos or a favourite blanket, can help them feel more grounded. At night, keep a light on so they can see where they are and what time it is if they wake up suddenly. These steps do not replace medical assessment if confusion is sudden or worsening; they are there to keep the person safer and more comfortable while professionals investigate and treat any underlying causes.
source: North Bristol NHS Trust, Patient Information - Delirium leaflet

title: Calm communication, reassurance and reorientation
content: When a person has delirium or sudden confusion, communication that feels calm and simple can reduce distress. Use short, straightforward sentences and avoid giving too much information at once, checking gently that they have understood. Offer reassurance by using a soft tone of voice, sitting at their level if possible, and reminding them that they are safe and not alone. Listen carefully, even if what they say seems muddled or based on frightening dreams or visions, and respond to the feelings rather than arguing with the details. If they are disoriented, quietly restate where they are, why they are there, and who the people around them are. Point out a visible clock or calendar and mention the date, time of day, and whether it is day or night. Encourage use of glasses and hearing aids so they can better follow conversations and their surroundings. Try to have familiar faces present, especially in the evening, as this may help them feel more settled when confusion often worsens. Bring familiar items from home that you can refer to during conversation, such as family photos, to anchor them in people and memories they recognise. If you are worried that their confusion is new, getting worse, or very different from their usual dementia, let ward staff or a clinician know promptly, while continuing these gentle communication strategies.
source: North Bristol NHS Trust, Patient Information - Delirium leaflet

title: Working with staff and when to seek help
content: Practical comfort measures are important, but sudden or worsening confusion always needs attention from health professionals. If the person is already in hospital, ward staff should be your first point of contact for questions or concerns about delirium or changes in behaviour. You can share what is normal for the person and any sudden changes you have noticed, and help staff complete tools like a personal information document so they can tailor care when you are not there. Ask staff how you can best support eating, drinking, and orientation alongside their medical plan. If the person is at home and you notice a sudden change in their confusion, alert a healthcare professional such as their GP or an urgent care service so the cause can be assessed and treated. Practical steps like reassurance, familiar objects, good lighting, and help with food and fluids are helpful, but they do not replace medical assessment when confusion is new, sudden, or significantly worse than usual. After a hospital stay with delirium, check what has been written or sent to the GP about the episode so you can request a follow-up review if needed. You can also contact local carer or dementia organisations, such as carers support services or the Alzheimer’s Society, for additional emotional and practical help for you as a caregiver.
source: North Bristol NHS Trust, Patient Information - Delirium leaflet

title: Delirium: key signs for carers
content: Delirium is described as an acute confusional state that usually develops over hours or a few days, often as a complication of illness or surgery, and is different from dementia, although people with dementia are more likely to develop it when unwell. People with delirium may struggle to concentrate, seem confused or disorientated, and may have hallucinations, such as seeing or hearing things that are not there. They can appear very restless, hyperactive and agitated, which can be frightening, or they may become very quiet, apathetic and withdrawn instead. These changes are out of character and can be very upsetting for families, especially if the person does not recognise them or behaves in ways they never would normally. Because delirium comes on quickly and is linked to physical illness or other medical problems, it should always be taken seriously and mentioned to staff if noticed in hospital. The leaflet also notes that some people, particularly those who are frail or already living with dementia, may not fully return to their previous level of functioning even after the causes of the delirium have been treated.
source: Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, "Information for carers about delirium"
licence: check before redistribution

title: How delirium can change and fluctuate
content: The leaflet explains that delirium symptoms usually appear over a short period of time (hours or a few days) and tend to fluctuate in how severe they are during the day. A person may seem more settled, alert and themselves at one point, then become more confused, disorientated, agitated or withdrawn later on the same day. Symptoms are often worse at night, a pattern sometimes called ‘sundowning’, where confusion and distress become more noticeable as evening and night-time approach. Carers are advised that this up-and-down pattern, with times of relative clarity and times of marked confusion, is a characteristic feature of delirium and can continue for some time, even after the original causes start to be treated. The leaflet compares recovery to jet-lag: even when the underlying problem is addressed, it can take a while for the brain to fully readjust, and some people may have lingering symptoms for a long time. Because delirium can come on quickly and change noticeably over a day, sudden or fluctuating confusion should not be dismissed as normal ageing or usual dementia progression, and any new changes should be reported to healthcare staff.
source: Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, "Information for carers about delirium"
licence: check before redistribution

title: Practical guidance for carers when delirium is suspected
content: The leaflet advises carers to tell hospital staff if they notice a change in their relative’s behaviour, such as sudden confusion, difficulty concentrating, hallucinations, or unusual agitation or withdrawal, because these may be signs of delirium that need medical assessment. It explains that doctors and nurses will look for underlying causes, such as infection, medication side effects, dehydration, pain, or heart, lung or brain problems, and start appropriate treatment while also using nursing approaches to reduce confusion and agitation. Carers can help by visiting regularly, bringing familiar objects, and using a calm, reassuring tone to talk about simple, non-threatening topics, while reminding the person gently where they are and what day and time it is. They are encouraged to keep visits short and avoid multiple visitors, loud chatter or overstimulation, which can increase confusion and distress. If the person has hallucinations, carers are advised to explain calmly that these are not real and avoid arguing if the person insists they are, as this can make things worse. The leaflet recognises how distressing delirium can be for families and encourages carers to discuss any difficulties with ward staff and work together with the team, including later explaining and reassuring the person about what happened once they begin to recover.
source: Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, "Information for carers about delirium"
licence: check before redistribution

title: Dementia: gradual, long-term changes
content:
This page explains that dementia is a long-term condition caused by damage to brain cells that affects memory, thinking, and problem-solving over time. It is described as chronic and progressive, meaning it is life-long and usually gets gradually worse rather than appearing suddenly. The person’s consciousness or basic level of awareness is not affected by dementia in the way it is by some other conditions. The most noticeable change is often increasing difficulty with memory, but other areas such as thinking through tasks, managing daily activities, or following conversations can also be affected. These changes tend to build up over months or years rather than hours or days.

For family caregivers, this means that a person with dementia may show a fairly consistent pattern of difficulties and needs, even if they slowly worsen. You may notice familiar problems, like repeated questions or needing more help with everyday tasks, that fit the picture of their usual dementia. When describing concerns to a GP or nurse, it can help to explain how long you have noticed the difficulties, how they have changed over months, and what the person’s usual or baseline memory and thinking are like. Emphasising that these difficulties have developed gradually over time can help clinicians distinguish long-term dementia symptoms from more sudden problems.
source: West Hertfordshire Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust - Dementia and Delirium service page
licence: check before redistribution

title: Delirium: sudden or acute confusion
content:
The page describes delirium as a neuropsychiatric syndrome often seen in hospital that is referred to as "acute confusion", meaning it is new and has come on very quickly. Unlike dementia’s slow progression, delirium usually develops over a short period, such as hours or days, and is linked with a sudden change from the person’s usual thinking or behaviour. The exact cause is not fully understood, but the page notes risk factors such as recent surgery, underlying medical conditions like dementia, constipation, and infections such as pneumonia or urinary tract infections. Poor sleep and chronic fatigue in hospital can also contribute.

For caregivers, key points are that delirium is about a noticeable and often rapid change compared with the person’s normal state, even if they already have dementia. You might suddenly see much more confusion, disorientation, or a sharp change in behaviour that feels different from their usual pattern. This type of change can signal a medical problem that needs timely attention, so it is important to raise concerns promptly with hospital staff, a GP, or another clinician. When you seek help, try to explain when you first noticed the change, how quickly it developed, and how this new confusion differs from their usual dementia-related difficulties.
source: West Hertfordshire Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust - Dementia and Delirium service page
licence: check before redistribution

title: Helping clinicians distinguish baseline dementia from acute change
content:
This source highlights that dementia is a chronic, progressive condition with symptoms that build gradually, while delirium is described as acute confusion that appears suddenly and often in the context of illness, surgery, or other medical stress. For caregivers speaking to a GP or nurse, it can help to describe the person’s usual or baseline dementia pattern first, including long-standing memory problems, thinking difficulties, and how these affect daily life over months or years. Then, clearly explain any new or different changes that appeared quickly, such as a sudden increase in confusion, disorientation, or unusual behaviour that does not fit their normal pattern.

The page notes that delirium is associated with underlying medical causes and risk factors such as dementia, infections like pneumonia or UTIs, constipation, recent surgery, and poor sleep or fatigue in hospital. While it does not list specific behaviours like agitation, withdrawal, or hallucinations, the emphasis on new and rapid confusion suggests that sudden changes in awareness or behaviour should be taken seriously and assessed by a clinician. When seeking help, caregivers can mention any recent operations, infections, bowel problems, or disturbed sleep, as well as the timing of changes in confusion. This information supports clinicians in assessing whether the change could be delirium on top of dementia or part of the person’s longer-term dementia progression.
source: West Hertfordshire Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust - Dementia and Delirium service page
licence: check before redistribution

title: NICE NG97 dementia recommendations - overview for family caregivers
content: This guideline sets out how health and social care services in England and Wales should assess, support and treat people living with dementia and those who care for them. It stresses that people have a right to be involved in decisions about their care and to receive information in ways they can understand, following national standards on accessible information, consent, mental capacity and safeguarding. After diagnosis, the person and their family should be given clear oral and written information about the type of dementia, what changes to expect, who is involved in their care, legal rights (including driving, work and reasonable adjustments) and how to contact local and national support, financial and legal advice and advocacy services. The guideline says people should have access to specialist memory or dementia services and that care should be coordinated by a named professional who organises assessments, shares information, develops and reviews a care and support plan and involves family or carers where appropriate. It covers non‑drug and drug treatments, managing distress, depression, sleep, other long‑term conditions, risks around hospital admission, palliative care, support for carers, moves between care settings and staff training. As a caregiver, you can use this to ask what services are available, who your named contact is, and how your family member’s rights and preferences are being taken into account.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations section

title: Involving the person with dementia and planning ahead
content: The guideline emphasises that people living with dementia should be encouraged and enabled to express their own views and preferences about care, using adapted communication methods and tools that capture their likes, dislikes, routines and personal history. Information for the person and their family or carers should match their situation and stage of dementia and be provided in accessible formats, including at diagnosis when they should receive clear explanations of their dementia subtype, expected changes, care team contacts, legal rights, work-related adjustments and relevant support organisations. Services should ask for consent to share information, record who the person wants involved, what can be shared, and ensure people know how to get more information if needs change. The guideline encourages early and ongoing **advance** care planning conversations about the benefits of planning ahead, lasting power of attorney, advance statements of wishes and values, advance decisions to refuse treatment, and preferences for place of care and death, with opportunities to review and change these at each care review. As a caregiver, you can use this to request time with clinicians to discuss communication needs, to ask for tools that record what matters to the person, and to bring up advance planning as a routine, revisited topic rather than a one‑off conversation.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations section 1.1
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Care coordination, care plans and navigating services
content: NICE NG97 states that each person living with dementia should have a single named health or social care professional responsible for coordinating their care. This person should arrange a face‑to‑face assessment where possible, explain available services and access routes, involve family or carers in support and decisions (in line with the Mental Capacity Act), and ensure people are aware of advocacy options when needed. They should lead on developing a written care and support plan that is agreed with the person and, where appropriate, family or carers, sets review timings, tracks progress, includes management of other health conditions and is shared with the person and those they want involved. The guideline stresses that services should be designed to be accessible to people without carers, people with limited transport or other responsibilities, people with disabilities and people from minority ethnic groups. Information such as care plans and advance care plans should transfer smoothly between home, hospital, community and care homes, with staff aiming for continuity and consistency of care. After any transition between settings, the person’s needs, wishes and plans should be reviewed. As a caregiver, you can ask who the named coordinator is, request a copy of the care plan, check how it will be shared across settings, and raise any barriers to attending appointments or using services.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations section 1.3 and 1.12
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Supporting and involving family carers
content: The guideline recognises family and unpaid carers as key partners in dementia care and recommends that their involvement in information sharing and decisions should be based on the person’s consent and preferences, recorded in the clinical record. Carers should receive psychoeducation and skills training that covers understanding dementia and its progression, developing personalised strategies, responding to changes in behaviour, adapting communication, looking after their own physical and mental health and wellbeing, planning meaningful shared activities, and finding and using local support and psychological services. Support for carers should be tailored to their goals and circumstances, designed to help them support the person with dementia, offered in accessible locations and formats (such as group, individual or online) and available from diagnosis onwards as needs change. Carers should be informed about their rights to a carer’s assessment, respite and other support under relevant legislation, and professionals should be aware that carers have an increased risk of depression and should follow guidance on identifying and managing this. The guideline notes that carer programmes are often most effective when delivered as group sessions, and suggests considering opportunities for carers to join staff dementia training so they better understand care approaches and communication strategies. As a caregiver, you can ask clinicians or coordinators about carer training offers, carer assessments, respite options and emotional support, and highlight your own support needs as part of routine care discussions.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations section 1.1, 1.3, 1.11 and 1.13
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Person-centred dementia care and shared decisions
content: NICE NG97 emphasises that people living with dementia should be encouraged and supported to share their own views and wishes about care, rather than others speaking for them wherever possible. Professionals are advised to adapt communication, for example by using visual aids, simpler text or structured tools that record the person’s likes, dislikes, routines and personal history, so that care fits who they are as an individual. Information for the person and, where appropriate, family members or carers should be matched to their situation and stage of dementia, and provided in accessible formats in line with the NHS Accessible Information Standard. At diagnosis, the person and their family or carers should be offered clear oral and written information about the type of dementia, what changes they might expect, which professionals and teams will be involved, how to contact them, how dementia may affect driving and work, and how to find local support, financial and legal advice and advocacy. The guideline encourages early and ongoing conversations about planning ahead, including the benefits of thinking about the future, lasting power of attorney, advance statements of wishes and values, advance decisions to refuse treatment and preferred place of care and death, with chances to review these at each care review. For caregivers, this means you can ask professionals to speak directly with the person, to adapt how they communicate, and to schedule time for regular, revisited planning discussions rather than one-off decisions.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations sections 1.1 and 1.10
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Involving and supporting family carers in dementia care
content: NICE NG97 treats family and unpaid carers as key partners in care, but stresses that their involvement should follow the person’s consent and preferences. At or soon after diagnosis, professionals should ask the person with dementia whether they agree to information being shared, who with, and what type of information can be shared, and record this clearly in their notes. People and carers should be told how to get more information and where to go for support as needs change, including how to access memory services or equivalent multidisciplinary dementia services. The guideline recommends that carers are offered psychoeducation and skills training that covers understanding dementia and its progression, personalised strategies for day-to-day care, how to respond to behaviour changes, adapting communication, maintaining their own wellbeing, planning meaningful activities together, and finding local and psychological support services. Support for carers should be tailored to their goals and preferences, offered in practical locations and formats, and available from diagnosis onwards. Carers should also be informed about their right to a carer’s assessment, respite and other support, and professionals should recognise their increased risk of depression and act in line with relevant mental health guidance. As a caregiver, you can ask the team to note your involvement preferences, request carer training, and raise your own support needs as an expected part of good dementia care, while still keeping the person living with dementia at the centre of decisions.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations sections 1.1 and 1.11

title: Care coordination, consent and caregiver oversight
content: NICE NG97 recommends that every person living with dementia has a single named health or social care professional responsible for coordinating their care. This coordinator should arrange a face‑to‑face needs assessment where possible, explain available services and access routes, involve family members or carers in support and decisions where appropriate, and give special consideration to people who may lack capacity in line with the Mental Capacity Act. They should also ensure that people know about advocacy services and, if needed, access to independent mental capacity advocates. The coordinator leads on creating a written care and support plan that is agreed and regularly reviewed with the person, and where appropriate their family or carers, including when and how often it will be reviewed, progress towards goals and how other health conditions are managed. With the person’s consent, care and support plans and any advance care plans should be shareable between home, hospital, community and care home settings, and staff should work to maximise continuity and consistency of care. Services should also be designed to be accessible to people who do not have carers, have transport or other responsibilities, have disabilities or come from minority ethnic groups. For caregivers, this framework supports oversight: you can ask who the named coordinator is, request copies of plans, check how information is shared between services, and raise concerns if decisions are being made without involving the person or considering capacity and consent.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations section 1.3

title: Medicines review and safety in dementia care
content: NICE NG97 explains that medicines for dementia should be started and reviewed under the guidance of clinicians with appropriate expertise, following wider medicines-optimisation guidance. For dementia drugs such as acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and memantine, starting treatment should usually be advised by a specialist (for example from a memory clinic, psychiatry, neurology or geriatrics), with local arrangements in place for prescribing and treatment review. The guideline advises that treatment decisions should not be based only on a single test score and should consider the whole person, including disabilities, communication needs and cultural background. It also states that medicines linked with increased anticholinergic burden can contribute to cognitive problems, and that clinicians should consider reducing or finding alternatives to these medicines when assessing suspected dementia and during medication reviews. There are validated tools to estimate anticholinergic burden, although no single tool is recommended over others. For caregivers, this means you are encouraged to ask for a structured medication review with a GP, pharmacist or memory service if you worry that medicines may be affecting memory, alertness or day-to-day functioning, but any decisions about starting, stopping or changing medicines should be made by qualified professionals.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations sections 1.5 and 1.6
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Professional oversight of dementia medicines and distress
content: NICE NG97 sets out that pharmacological treatments for dementia and related symptoms should only be used under professional oversight and after careful assessment. For distress, agitation, aggression and psychosis, the guideline recommends a structured assessment first to look for underlying causes such as pain, delirium or environmental factors, and to prioritise psychosocial and environmental approaches. Antipsychotic medicines should only be considered when a person is at risk of harming themselves or others, or when severe agitation, hallucinations or delusions cause significant distress, and they should be used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest possible time with regular (at least six‑weekly) reviews. Decisions about starting or continuing antipsychotics should involve a discussion with the person and, where appropriate, family or carers, supported by decision aids produced by NICE. The guideline also warns that people with dementia with Lewy bodies or Parkinson’s disease dementia may be particularly sensitive to antipsychotics and may become worse. It advises that some medicines such as valproate and melatonin should not be used for agitation or sleep problems in dementia unless indicated for another condition or as part of a specific safety framework. For caregivers, this supports raising any concerns about new or worsening distress, side effects or sedation with a GP, specialist or pharmacist rather than adjusting doses yourselves.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations sections 1.5 and 1.7
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: When caregivers should raise medication concerns
content: NICE NG97 describes several situations where review of medicines and wider treatment by professionals is important and where caregivers’ observations are valuable. Medicines that increase anticholinergic burden can worsen cognition, so clinicians are advised to consider minimising them during dementia assessments and routine medication reviews. When a person shows new or worsening agitation, aggression, hallucinations, sleep problems or mood changes, the guideline recommends a structured clinical review to check for causes such as pain, delirium, depression, inappropriate care or side effects before starting or changing medicines. It also advises repeating pain and symptom assessments after any pain-management intervention and being aware of carers’ increased risk of depression. For dementia drugs and for treatments of other long-term conditions, NG97 emphasises equivalent access to care and the need to balance benefits and harms, often using separate condition-specific NICE guidance. For caregivers, practical triggers to contact a GP, pharmacist or memory clinic include sudden changes in alertness or confusion, new falls, marked sedation, visible distress, or doubts about whether medicines are still helping. You can prepare by keeping a brief symptom diary, listing all prescribed and over-the-counter medicines, and asking structured questions such as: “Could any of these medicines be making memory or confusion worse?” or “Is it time for a formal medication review?” while leaving prescribing decisions to clinicians.
source: NICE guideline NG97 recommendations sections 1.5, 1.6, 1.7 and 1.8

title: Recognising and understanding distress in dementia
content:
People living with dementia can show distress in many ways, including increased aggression, anxiety, apathy, agitation, depression, delusions, hallucinations and changes in sleep. These behaviours are not always caused by dementia alone and may be linked to other problems such as pain, infection, delirium, discomfort or care that does not meet the person’s needs. Before any treatment is started, health and care professionals are expected to carry out a structured assessment to look for possible reasons behind the distress, rather than focusing only on the behaviour itself.

Family members and carers may be involved in these checks, especially when the person has difficulty communicating or lacks capacity, because they can share what is usual for the person and what has changed. A structured assessment looks at clinical causes (such as pain or infection), environmental factors (like noise, temperature or overstimulation), medication issues, communication difficulties and sensory problems. For caregivers, a practical way to support this is to calmly observe and note what was happening before, during and after an episode of distress, including where it occurred, who was present, what the person might have been trying to express, and any changes in routine, health or environment. These observations can help professionals identify and address underlying causes, and may reduce the need for unnecessary medical interventions.
source: NICE Quality Standard QS184, Quality statement 6: Managing distress
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Non-pharmacological approaches and environment for distress
content:
NICE highlights that non-pharmacological treatments should be considered for distress in dementia and may include psychosocial and environmental interventions, as well as personalised activities that promote engagement, pleasure and interest. Examples include calming and distracting the person when they are agitated, adjusting the environment (for instance, changing temperature, reducing noise or improving lighting), and offering meaningful activities such as arts and crafts, movement to music or accompanied walks. These approaches aim to reduce distress by addressing comfort, boredom, overstimulation, or lack of meaningful occupation, rather than immediately turning to medication.

Clinical guidance also notes that anxiety and depression in people with dementia may sometimes be addressed with approaches such as cognitive behavioural therapy, multisensory stimulation, relaxation techniques and animal-assisted therapies, delivered by appropriate professionals. For unpaid family caregivers, a practical focus is on creating a calm, predictable environment, noticing whether changes in noise, crowding, lighting, temperature, or routine seem to make things better or worse, and gently trying soothing activities the person usually enjoys. Caregivers can record which environmental changes or activities appear to reduce distress and share this with professionals, supporting more tailored, non-pharmacological care plans. Decisions about any pharmacological treatments, including antipsychotics, antidepressants or pain relief, should be made by healthcare professionals following structured assessment.
source: NICE Quality Standard QS184, Quality statement 6: Managing distress
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Caregiver role in structured assessment and communication needs
content:
NICE states that before any non-pharmacological or pharmacological treatment for distress is started, people with dementia should have a structured assessment to explore reasons for their distress. Health and social care professionals are expected to check for clinical and environmental causes, including pain, discomfort, delirium, inappropriate care, infection, boredom, noise or temperature, and to involve family members or carers when needed. Caregivers can support this by describing how the person usually behaves, what has changed, and any patterns they have noticed, such as specific times of day, situations or triggers.

The guidance emphasises that people with dementia may have difficulties communicating because of cognitive problems, learning disabilities or language barriers and that professionals should identify any speech, language or other communication needs. They should also find out the person’s preferred way of communicating and whether they want someone important to them present when discussing their distress; other methods such as visual aids or simplified text may be used if helpful. For caregivers, this means paying attention to how the person best understands information (for example, short simple sentences, gestures, pictures) and supporting communication in a respectful, non-judgemental way. Sharing these insights with professionals can help ensure that discussions about distress and next steps are more comfortable, understandable and person-centred.
source: NICE Quality Standard QS184, Quality statement 6: Managing distress
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: When antipsychotic medicines may be considered in dementia
content: This decision aid explains that people living with dementia can sometimes become very agitated, aggressive, or distressed, and may experience hallucinations (seeing or hearing things that are not there) or delusions (strong beliefs that are not true).  Before antipsychotic medicines are considered, several non-medicine approaches should usually be tried, such as music, exercise, or aromatherapy, to help calm the person.  NICE recommends that an antipsychotic is only tried if the person is at risk of harming themselves or others, or is severely distressed, and it should always be used alongside other supportive activities rather than on its own.  The decision to start or continue an antipsychotic is made by healthcare professionals in discussion with the person (where possible) and their carers, because it involves weighing up potential benefits and serious risks.  The medicine should be used at the lowest dose that helps and for the shortest possible time, and the person should be assessed at least every 6 weeks so that the medicine can be stopped if it is not helping or is no longer needed.  The decision aid emphasises that the person does not have to have an antipsychotic; there are pros and cons, and these should be carefully discussed with a GP, specialist, or other member of the care team rather than decided by caregivers alone.
source: NICE NG97 antipsychotic medicines decision aid for treating agitation, aggression and distress in people living with dementia
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Benefits and risks of antipsychotic medicines in dementia
content: According to this decision aid, many people who take an antipsychotic for agitation, aggression, or psychotic symptoms in dementia will become less agitated and are less likely to harm themselves or others.  Hallucinations and delusions may become less troubling or may stop, although it can be hard to judge this if the person has difficulties communicating.  However, antipsychotics can cause side effects such as feeling sleepy or less alert, headaches, changes in appetite, weight gain, and movement problems similar to Parkinson’s disease (for example, stiffness, slowness, tremor, or a shuffling walk).  These side effects are more likely at higher doses and with longer use, and there are additional, less common side effects that a healthcare professional can explain.  Importantly, there is an increased risk of stroke and an increased risk of death while taking an antipsychotic over 6 to 12 weeks; for every 1,000 people with dementia taking an antipsychotic, about 12 extra people may have a stroke and about 11 extra people may die because they take the medicine, compared with not taking it.  The decision aid stresses that it is not possible to predict exactly what will happen for any individual person, so benefits and risks must always be discussed with clinicians and monitored carefully over time.
source: NICE NG97 antipsychotic medicines decision aid for treating agitation, aggression and distress in people living with dementia
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Shared decision-making, review, and caregiver questions about antipsychotics
content: This decision aid is designed to support conversations between people living with dementia, their family members or carers, and healthcare professionals about whether to use an antipsychotic medicine.  It highlights that antipsychotics are usually taken as a tablet or liquid once or twice a day, and that only risperidone (for up to 6 weeks) and haloperidol are formally licensed for these symptoms in dementia; using other antipsychotics or using risperidone for longer than 6 weeks is considered "off-label" and should be explained by the prescriber.  The aid recommends that the person is reviewed at least every 6 weeks to see whether the medicine is still needed, whether it is helping, and whether side effects or risks outweigh the benefits, with the option to stop the medicine if it is not helping or no longer needed.  Caregivers can use this information to prepare questions for clinicians, such as: what non-medicine approaches have been tried; why an antipsychotic is being suggested now; which medicine is planned and for how long; what benefits to look for; what side effects or warning signs to watch for; and how often the treatment will be reviewed.  The aid reinforces that caregivers should not change medicines themselves, but should raise any concerns about agitation, aggression, distress, or possible side effects promptly with a GP, psychiatrist, pharmacist, or memory clinic team.
source: NICE NG97 antipsychotic medicines decision aid for treating agitation, aggression and distress in people living with dementia
licence: NICE content - check NICE reuse terms before redistribution

title: Dementia medicines and who should prescribe
content:
This summary explains, in plain English, how dementia medicines are usually started and reviewed, and why decisions are led by specialists and other clinicians rather than family caregivers. For Alzheimer’s disease, medicines that aim to support memory and thinking are usually started only on the advice of a specialist clinician experienced in dementia care, such as a memory clinic doctor. In some situations, particular medicines may later be started or continued in primary care, but this still happens under clear guidance from specialists and within local NHS arrangements.

For non-Alzheimer’s dementias, the guidance explains that some dementia medicines are used "off-licence" and only in specific situations, for example when there is mixed dementia or dementia with Lewy bodies, and that they are not recommended for some types, such as frontotemporal dementia. This means treatment choices are nuanced and depend on the dementia type and co-existing conditions, so caregivers are encouraged to raise questions with the GP, memory clinic, or specialist nurse rather than asking for a particular drug by name. When preparing for an appointment, caregivers might bring notes about changes in memory, daily functioning, mood, sleep, or behaviour, and any concerns about how current medicines are tolerated, so that the clinician can review treatment and monitoring safely.
source: BNF dementia treatment summary
licence: BNF/NICE content - check reuse terms before redistribution

title: Medicines that may worsen confusion or cause harm
content:
The BNF dementia summary highlights that some commonly prescribed medicines can worsen confusion or thinking problems because they add to the "anticholinergic burden" (sometimes called antimuscarinic burden). These include certain antidepressants, antihistamines, antipsychotics, and medicines used for bladder symptoms, and the guidance states that their use should be minimised in people with dementia where possible. For caregivers, this means that if a person’s memory, alertness, or confusion has worsened after a medicine was started or changed, it is reasonable to ask the GP or pharmacist to review whether any drugs could be contributing, rather than stopping anything themselves.

The summary also explains that antipsychotic medicines should only be considered when the person with dementia is at risk of harming themselves or others, or if severe agitation, hallucinations, or delusions are causing major distress, and even then they should be used at the lowest effective dose, for the shortest possible time, with regular review at least every 6 weeks. There is an increased risk of stroke and a small increased risk of death when antipsychotics are used in older people with dementia, so clinicians need to balance potential benefits and risks carefully, especially if there is a history of stroke or vascular risk factors such as high blood pressure, diabetes, smoking, or atrial fibrillation. Caregivers can support safe use by asking how long an antipsychotic is intended for, how often it will be reviewed, what signs of side effects to watch for (such as drowsiness, worsening mobility, or stroke symptoms), and what non-drug strategies are also being tried.
source: BNF dementia treatment summary
licence: BNF/NICE content - check reuse terms before redistribution

title: Treatment review, non-drug options and monitoring in dementia
content:
The BNF dementia summary emphasises that treatment aims to promote independence, maintain function, and manage symptoms, and that non-drug approaches are an important part of care alongside any medicines. For cognitive symptoms, people with mild-to-moderate dementia should be offered structured group cognitive stimulation programmes, and group reminiscence, cognitive rehabilitation, or occupational therapy may also be considered to support everyday functioning. These interventions are usually arranged by clinicians or dementia services, so caregivers can ask the GP or memory clinic whether such activities are available locally and whether a referral is appropriate.

For non-cognitive symptoms such as agitation, distress, psychosis, depression, anxiety, or sleep disturbance, the guidance highlights that psychosocial and environmental measures should be tried, including counselling, addressing pain or delirium, psychological therapies, sleep hygiene, exposure to daylight, and increasing exercise and activity, before or alongside medicines. Antidepressants are reserved mainly for people with pre-existing severe mental health problems, and sleeping tablets are not presented as a first-line solution. Caregivers can help by observing and noting triggers for distress, mood changes, or poor sleep, and by bringing this information to appointments so clinicians can consider both non-drug strategies and any necessary medication review. The summary also signposts NICE guideline NG97 on dementia for further guidance on assessment, management, and support for people living with dementia and their carers, which clinicians may use when planning care.
source: BNF dementia treatment summary
licence: BNF/NICE content - check reuse terms before redistribution

title: Eating and drinking difficulties in dementia
content: This leaflet explains that people with dementia often have difficulties with eating and drinking, which can lead to weight loss, malnutrition, dehydration, chest infections and reduced enjoyment of food and drink. It lists common challenges such as forgetting to eat or drink, not recognising food or drink, struggling to concentrate on meals, holding food or drink in the mouth, coughing when eating or drinking, changes in taste or smell, food left in the mouth after eating, difficulty using cutlery or cups, difficulty chewing, refusing food and having a reduced appetite. For carers, it stresses that these changes are common in dementia and may need practical support and professional input, rather than blame or pressure on the person. It also highlights that different professionals can help: speech and language therapists can assess swallowing and suggest safe strategies; occupational therapists can advise on aids and positioning; dietitians can support with nutrition, supplements and appetite; and doctors can advise on problems swallowing tablets and refer on. Carers are encouraged to seek help from these professionals if they are worried about safety, swallowing, weight loss, poor intake or how to manage mealtimes.
source: Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust patient information leaflet 'Eating and drinking difficulties in Dementia'
licence: check before redistribution

title: Practical mealtime support strategies for dementia
content: The leaflet gives practical ideas for carers to make eating and drinking easier and calmer for someone with dementia. Before meals, it suggests making sure the person is fully awake, sitting upright as close to 90 degrees as possible, and wearing their glasses and hearing aids so they can see and hear well. Good mouth care is encouraged by checking dentures fit properly and brushing teeth and gums after eating. The environment should be as calm as possible: reduce distractions such as television, make mealtimes social by eating together, ensure food and drink are in the person’s eyeline and easy to reach, and allow plenty of time so they can eat at their own pace. If someone has difficulty feeding themselves, carers can offer finger foods like sandwiches or vegetable sticks, use transparent cups or glasses, try using a spoon, give gentle hand-over-hand assistance by guiding the person’s hand, and help cut up food if needed. These approaches aim to support independence where possible while giving enough help to keep mealtimes safe and less stressful.
source: Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust patient information leaflet 'Eating and drinking difficulties in Dementia'
licence: check before redistribution

title: Encouraging appetite and hydration in dementia
content: The leaflet suggests several ways carers can gently encourage eating and drinking when appetite or interest in food is reduced. It advises making food look and smell appealing, for example by presenting foods carefully and using moulds for pureed meals, and focusing on foods the person enjoys because tastes can change over time. Carers are encouraged to offer foods with high water content such as jelly, soups and gravy, aim for smaller, more frequent meals instead of large portions, and provide snacks and drinks regularly throughout the day to support hydration and intake. Involving the person is important: give simple choices, using pictures or objects if helpful; wait until the person is calm before offering food and drink; watch for non-verbal cues such as body language and eye contact; and ask about favourite foods where possible. The leaflet also notes that speech and language therapists, occupational therapists, dietitians and doctors can help if there are concerns about swallowing, nutrition, appetite, or safely managing food and drink. Carers are encouraged to raise worries about coughing on food or drink, food being held in the mouth, poor intake, or weight loss with these professionals so safe, tailored advice can be given.
source: Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust patient information leaflet 'Eating and drinking difficulties in Dementia'
licence: check before redistribution

title: Eating support for people with dementia: small meals, familiar foods and mealtime environment
content: This NHS leaflet explains that as dementia progresses, the main goal of eating and drinking support is to prioritise the person’s comfort, enjoyment and quality of life rather than strict healthy-eating rules. Caregivers are encouraged to offer foods and drinks the person enjoys, including sweeter options if that is their preference, and to remember likes, dislikes and usual routines while also being open to new preferences. If someone struggles to sit for a full meal, smaller meals ‘little and often’ (for example six smaller meals a day) and a variety of finger foods that can be eaten on the move can be helpful. The leaflet suggests keeping the mealtime environment calm and familiar, clearing clutter, reducing background noise such as TV, and considering calming music. Eating together so the person is not alone, allowing extra time, and offering practical help with eating and drinking (for example cutting food up, using plate warmers or insulated cups) are recommended. Caregivers are advised to ask where the person feels most comfortable sitting, ensure they have used the toilet and are ready for a meal, and to make the most of times when they naturally want to eat, even if that is at night. The leaflet reassures caregivers not to feel disheartened if food or drink is refused, and to try again later or offer an alternative instead of forcing or pressuring the person.
source: Buckinghamshire, Oxfordshire and Berkshire West Integrated Care Board (ICB) leaflet "Support with eating and drinking for people with dementia" Version 1.0, November 2023.
licence: check before redistribution

title: Practical strategies for low appetite, distress and practical difficulties at mealtimes
content: The leaflet gives calm, practical suggestions for when a person with dementia shows no interest in meals, refuses food, becomes distressed, or pushes food away. Caregivers are encouraged to check for treatable problems such as toothache, sore gums, oral thrush, a dry mouth, constipation or infections, and to make sure mouth care is completed at least twice daily. It suggests being led by what the person wants to eat, even if combinations seem unusual, and trying different routines such as a larger lunch and lighter evening meal if this suits them. Involving the person in choosing, preparing and smelling food may help appetite and positive associations, and caregivers are advised to keep portions small, focus on ‘little and often’, and ensure food looks and smells appealing. Where someone struggles to chew or swallow their usual diet, the leaflet recommends verbal reminders to chew and swallow, offering softer or puréed textures, avoiding very dry foods or adding sauces, and ensuring the person is fully awake and sitting upright before food or drink. It also highlights checking that dentures fit and that mouth care is maintained, and advises asking the GP for a referral to a Speech and Language Therapist if the person is coughing or choking on food, rather than trying to manage swallowing difficulties alone. The leaflet also offers simple visual and environmental strategies such as picture menus, clear glasses, plain or high-contrast plates, and uncluttered tables to help the person recognise food and drink.
source: Buckinghamshire, Oxfordshire and Berkshire West Integrated Care Board (ICB) leaflet "Support with eating and drinking for people with dementia" Version 1.0, November 2023.
licence: check before redistribution

title: Hydration, remembering to eat, weight changes and when to seek professional help
content: The leaflet explains that poor eating and drinking can lead to problems such as pressure sores, falls, muscle weakness, fatigue, infections, constipation, dizziness, low blood pressure or sugar, and poor oral health, while too much weight gain can also affect health and quality of life. For someone who forgets to eat, it suggests using timers or reminders and keeping food and drink within sight and easy reach. To support fluid intake, caregivers are encouraged to offer a range of drinks throughout the day (such as milkshakes, smoothies, tea, coffee, squash, juice or water, at different temperatures) and to include foods with a high liquid content like soups, casseroles, sauces, yoghurt, jelly, ice lollies, melon or cucumber. Regular gentle prompting to drink, using reminder or tick charts, and drawing attention with a brightly coloured cup may help. Where someone struggles to use cutlery or drink from a cup, the leaflet advises verbal cues, placing utensils in the person’s hands, and considering adapted equipment such as wide-handled cutlery, handled beakers, non-slip or lipped plates, plate guards, and more finger foods. For over-eating or weight gain, it suggests checking whether the person is bored or lonely, offering smaller portions with extra salad or vegetables, choosing fruit and low-fat yoghurt for pudding, encouraging hydration, leaving small healthy snacks within reach, and using lower calorie foods and drinks. The leaflet advises that further support can be requested from a GP, who can assess mental health concerns, arrange a referral to a Dietitian if food-first strategies are not enough, or refer to Speech and Language Therapy if there is coughing or choking on food.
source: Buckinghamshire, Oxfordshire and Berkshire West Integrated Care Board (ICB) leaflet "Support with eating and drinking for people with dementia" Version 1.0, November 2023.
licence: check before redistribution

title: Nutrition and advanced dementia: common problems and everyday support
content: This leaflet explains that eating and drinking problems are common in people living with advanced dementia and often worsen as the condition progresses. It notes that the person may lose weight, have reduced appetite, or refuse food, and may struggle to recognise food, drinks, and items like plates, cups, knives, and forks. The leaflet highlights that pain, medication, mouth problems, constipation, and other medical issues can affect appetite, and that the medical team will look for these causes. It stresses how important it is for relatives and friends to notice changes, because the person with dementia may not be able to explain what is wrong or what they like. Practical suggestions include getting to know and sharing the person’s food likes and dislikes with staff; offering small, regular portions of favourite foods; trying foods with different colours, smells, textures, and possibly sweeter or stronger flavours; and keeping mealtimes relaxed and unhurried. Caregivers are encouraged to allow plenty of time, re-offer refused foods later, and make sure glasses, hearing aids, and dentures are in place for meals. The leaflet suggests helping with meals, making the most of times of day when appetite is better, chopping food so it can be eaten with a spoon or fingers, using easy-grip cutlery, and using brightly coloured plates to make food easier to see. It also reassures caregivers that it is acceptable to offer dessert even if the savoury meal is unfinished and not to worry about mixing sweet and savoury foods.
source: Leicester Hospitals NHS, Information for Patients, Families & Carers: Help and advice on nutrition for people living with advanced dementia (Leaflet 247, Version 3, last reviewed July 2025).
licence: Re-use of this leaflet is restricted by Creative Commons license. Check before redistribution.

title: Managing chewing, soft/moist foods, and hydration in advanced dementia
content: The leaflet explains that people living with dementia may have problems with chewing and swallowing, such as forgetting how to chew or holding food in their mouth. It emphasises the importance of good mouth care: keeping the mouth clean and comfortable can make food taste better and support chewing and swallowing, while painful mouths can make eating difficult. The leaflet advises checking that dentures or plates fit well, as weight loss can make them loose, and notes that denture fixative may help. It also acknowledges that people with advanced dementia can tire easily, so offering soft, moist foods that need little chewing may make eating less effortful. For drinking, the leaflet explains that the sense of thirst changes with age and dementia, so people may not realise they are thirsty. Caregivers are encouraged to prompt drinking throughout the day, using a "little and often" approach, and to offer drinks the person enjoys. It suggests using a clear glass so the person can see the drink or a brightly coloured cup to draw attention, and placing the cup in their hand or within reach, then drinking with them so they can copy. The leaflet advises that beakers should only be used if recommended by a speech and language therapist because of the risk of drinks entering the windpipe. It reminds caregivers that all fluids count, including soup, and that milk-based drinks such as hot chocolate, milkshakes, milky coffee or prescribed supplements can add extra calories and protein when food intake is low.
source: Leicester Hospitals NHS, Information for Patients, Families & Carers: Help and advice on nutrition for people living with advanced dementia (Leaflet 247, Version 3, last reviewed July 2025).
licence: Re-use of this leaflet is restricted by Creative Commons license. Check before redistribution.

title: Swallowing difficulties, unsafe swallow, and when to involve professionals
content: The leaflet describes how swallowing (dysphagia) can become more difficult as dementia progresses, because muscles and reflexes do not work properly and memory or behaviour changes affect how the person manages food and drink. It explains that people may hold food in their mouth, keep chewing without swallowing, or eventually lose the ability to swallow safely or at all. When swallowing changes, eating and drinking can become unsafe and may lead to choking or food and drink entering the windpipe and lungs (aspiration), which can cause serious chest infections such as pneumonia, weight loss, and dehydration. The leaflet states that if swallowing problems arise, the team will refer the person to a speech and language therapist, who will assess swallowing and advise on safe feeding, including drink thickness, food consistency, positioning, and pace of eating and drinking. It explains that, in some cases, speech and language therapists may judge all food and drink to be unsafe, resulting in an "unsafe swallow". The leaflet also notes that tube feeding is only considered if swallowing problems are thought to be reversible and that, in advanced dementia, tube feeding does not improve life expectancy, does not remove the risk of aspiration, and can be uncomfortable and distressing. When tube feeding is not advised, the team may discuss "at risk" or "comfort" feeding, where eating and drinking continue despite risks, guided by speech and language therapist advice on safest consistencies. Caregivers are encouraged to support this by providing regular mouth care, helping at mealtimes, ensuring the person is upright, awake and unrushed, letting them rest if they struggle, and thinking about what the person would have wanted in terms of comfort and quality of life as their condition progresses. The leaflet advises that if nutrition has become a worry, relatives should speak to the healthcare professionals looking after the person.
source: Leicester Hospitals NHS, Information for Patients, Families & Carers: Help and advice on nutrition for people living with advanced dementia (Leaflet 247, Version 3, last reviewed July 2025).
licence: Re-use of this leaflet is restricted by Creative Commons license. Check before redistribution.

title: How dementia can affect drinking and signs of dehydration
content: The Alzheimer’s Society explains that a person with dementia may not notice they are thirsty, may forget to drink, or may be unable to ask for a drink or get one for themselves, which can lead to dehydration. Dehydration can then cause problems such as headaches, greater confusion, constipation and urinary tract infections, which may worsen the person’s dementia symptoms. Caregivers are encouraged to watch for possible signs that the person is not taking in enough fluid, such as a dry mouth or lips, feeling dizzy or lightheaded, needing the toilet less often than usual, and having dark or strong-smelling urine. The page notes that simply placing a drink in front of the person does not guarantee they will drink it, and an empty cup does not always mean the person has finished the drink, as it might have been spilled, poured away or drunk by someone else. This information can help caregivers interpret what they see day-to-day and stay alert to early changes in fluid intake so they can gently offer support and seek advice if they are worried.
source: Alzheimer's Society - Drinking, hydration and dementia
licence: Alzheimer’s Society content - paraphrase/link-only unless reuse rights are checked

title: Practical ways to support hydration in dementia
content: The Alzheimer’s Society suggests several practical steps families can use to help someone with dementia stay hydrated throughout the day. Caregivers are encouraged to offer drinks regularly, aiming for multiple cups or glasses spread across the day, and to provide a variety of drinks such as tea, coffee, hot or cold milky drinks, fruit juice or smoothies, soup, squash and water so the person can choose what they like. The page recommends offering a drink whenever the person is eating and considering the type of cup or glass used; for example, using a clear glass so they can see the drink, or a brightly coloured cup to draw attention, and making sure it is easy to hold and not too heavy. It also suggests placing the drink where the person can easily see and reach it, or gently handing it to them, and using a straw if this makes drinking easier. Caregivers are also advised to include foods with a high liquid content such as gravy, ice lollies, milk jellies and yoghurt, and may consider specially designed sweets like Jelly Drops to help the person take in more water, if these suit the person’s needs and preferences.
source: Alzheimer's Society - Drinking, hydration and dementia
